ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has Chinese broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) as the closest parent; no distinct baby/young-shoot variant code exists. Baby Kailan is the immature shoot form of the same species.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers Chinese broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) as the closest parent category. No separate baby-shoot sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 412061001 represents Chinese broccoli as a substance; no distinct baby kailan concept exists. Used as closest parent.

main_ingredients: baby kailan (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra, young shoots)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of baby kailan in Singapore is approximately 200 g, presented as a plate of stir-fried or blanched young shoots dressed with garlic and oyster sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Broccoli (Gai Lan), cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11993 / FDC ID 169967)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Baby kailan is the immature shoot form of Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra (Chinese broccoli / Gai Lan). No dedicated baby-kailan entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked Chinese broccoli (FDC 169967), which is the closest approved-source match. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant preparation with oyster sauce and salt (estimated ~350 mg/100 g finished dish). Energy and macros are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo reference ranges for stir-fried leafy greens.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for leafy brassica vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baby kailan exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables (broccoli, Chinese broccoli, kale) are consistently reported with GI values in the range of 10–20 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (e.g., Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Foster-Powell K et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002). A GI of 15 is used as a defensible analogous estimate for baby kailan. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 7.0) / 100 = 1.05, rounded to 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from oyster sauce and added salt in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a restaurant serving of baby kailan can contribute approximately 700 mg sodium, and should request reduced sauce or opt for blanched preparation to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low net carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baby kailan is an excellent vegetable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI and minimal glycemic load, contributing negligibly to postprandial blood glucose when consumed in typical serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in dietary fibre, potassium, folate, and vitamin C with negligible saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baby kailan provides cardioprotective micronutrients including folate, potassium, and vitamin C; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-oil, low-sodium preparation to avoid excess sodium from oyster sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baby Kailan (小芥兰, Xiǎo Jiélán) is the young shoot form of Chinese broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra), a leafy green vegetable widely consumed across Singapore, Hong Kong, and southern China. The immature shoots are harvested before the plant fully matures, yielding tender stems and small, dark-green leaves with a mild, slightly bitter flavour and a crisp yet tender texture. In Singapore's Chinese restaurant cuisine, baby kailan is most commonly prepared by stir-frying or blanching and is typically served dressed with garlic, oyster sauce, and a drizzle of sesame or vegetable oil. The dish is a staple side vegetable in Cantonese and Singaporean Chinese dining, frequently ordered alongside rice dishes, roasted meats, and seafood. Nutritionally, baby kailan is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable providing approximately 35 kcal per 100 g. It is a good source of dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g), vitamin C (28 mg/100 g), folate (45 mcg DFE/100 g), calcium (72 mg/100 g), and vitamin A (48 mcg RAE/100 g). Its very low glycemic index (~15) makes it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content, which can be elevated in restaurant preparations due to oyster sauce and added salt, reaching approximately 350 mg/100 g or ~700 mg per typical 200 g serving. Individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sauce or blanched preparation. Baby kailan is halal-certified in most Singapore restaurants and is suitable for vegetarians when prepared without oyster sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in baby kailan?
Is baby kailan good for diabetics?
What is the difference between kailan and baby kailan?
Is baby kailan the same as Chinese broccoli?
How is baby kailan prepared in Singapore?
Is baby kailan high in sodium?
Can I eat baby kailan if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in baby kailan?

confidence_reason: No dedicated baby kailan entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked Chinese broccoli (FDC 169967), the closest approved-source match. Sodium is estimated upward to reflect typical restaurant preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for leafy brassica vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 169967 (cooked Chinese broccoli / Gai Lan) as no dedicated baby kailan entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. 2. Sodium_mg (350 mg/100 g) is an upward estimate from the base USDA value (~11 mg/100 g for plain cooked gai lan) to reflect typical Singapore restaurant preparation with oyster sauce and salt; this is consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo sodium ranges for stir-fried leafy greens with sauce (~300–400 mg/100 g). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for a plant-based vegetable dish with no hydrogenated fats; naturally zero by food composition. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components in the base vegetable. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a leafy green vegetable; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for leafy brassica vegetables from University of Sydney GI Database reference range (10–20 for non-starchy leafy greens); no direct baby kailan GI measurement found. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (15 × 7.0 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 1.05, reported as 1.1. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA FDC 169967 and are not -1 as the source reports these values. 10. Allergen note: standard restaurant preparation uses oyster sauce (mollusc-derived); vegan flag is false accordingly. 11. Food group reclassified from input 'Mixed' to 'Vegetables' as baby kailan is a leafy green vegetable dish.
